retrieved_docs.title: Osteoarthritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for osteoarthritis includes: Rheumatoid arthritis Psoriatic arthritis Crystalline arthritis Bursitis Tendinitis Hemochromatosis Avascular necrosis Radiculopathy Other soft-tissue conditions

retrieved_docs.title: Arthritis -- History and Physical -- 5. Pattern.
retrieved_docs.content: Other clinical features that can assist narrowing the differential diagnosis include family history and the age of onset with osteoarthritis more common in the older population while inflammatory arthritides more common in younger adults. It is also important to closely evaluate the neighboring joints to rule out referred pain. Skin examination is crucial and can greatly assist in diagnosis (eg. skin psoriasis, subcutaneous nodules, tophi, lupus rashes, sclerodactyly, etc.).

retrieved_docs.title: Arthritis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis is a broad and complex topic with many types, subtypes, and variations. The above is a summary of this expansive subject and does not aim to cover it in its entirety. It does, however, aim to provide an introductory synopsis of the arthritides and give the reader the knowledge to diagnose and treat this condition in most settings.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: With advanced disease, joint involvement on plain radiographs will reveal periarticular osteopenia, joint space narrowing, and bony erosions (marginal). Erosions of cartilage and bone are considered pathognomonic findings for RA, although not absolutely specific. However, these findings are consistent with advanced disease. [82] Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and ultrasonography are useful in early disease before radiographic evidence of bone erosion occurs. [83] A decreased signal from the bone marrow on T1-weighted images and gadolinium-enhanced images indicates bone marrow edema. MRI can also reveal synovial thickening, which has been shown to predict the future presence of bony erosions. [84] The clinical utility of MRI and its incorporation into the diagnostic criteria for RA remains to be determined.

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Arthritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: It is of importance to differentiate inflammatory from noninflammatory arthritis. The most common form of non-inflammatory arthritis is osteoarthritis which usually has an insidious onset and gradually worsens. Typical joints involved in primary osteoarthritis include the knees, hips, lumbar spine, 1st metatarsophalangeal joints in the feet, proximal and distal interphalangeal joints in the hands, and 1st carpometacarpal joint in the wrist. Repetitive use or injuries can lead to the involvement of other joints as well, such as the shoulders, elbows, ankles, and wrists. It can be symmetric or non-symmetric. Bony hypertrophy changes can be palpable on the exam, especially in the hands and feet. The joint can be tender and swollen, but erythema or warmth is usually absent. Synovial fluid analysis reveals noninflammatory fluid. Radiographs can show characteristic osteoarthritic changes with loss of joint space and osteophytes. Erosions are rare, but central "gull-wing" erosions can be seen in erosive osteoarthritis of the hands involving the proximal and distal interphalangeal joints.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Osteoarthritis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: No laboratory test can diagnose osteoarthritis, as the diagnosis is clinically based. Imaging can aid in the diagnosis of OA and can be used to exclude other types of arthritis. Inflammatory markers, including the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), are usually normal in OA but may be useful in ruling out other conditions when the diagnosis is uncertain. Additional laboratory testing can be employed to exclude inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, which may include a rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (CCP) antibodies. Genetic testing looking into aberrations in collagen formation and other collagen matrix proteins, which lead to premature OA, are currently employed by research facilities. Many other biomarkers are being investigated as novel techniques to diagnose early OA, monitor disease progression, and/or respond to interventions. More data is needed, however, to validate these methods.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the varied clinical presentation and lack of universal pathognomonic testing for RA, diagnosing the disease can be challenging early in the course of the disease. Traditionally the presence of at least four of the following criteria for at least six weeks would classify the patient as having RA. These criteria were: morning stiffness, arthritis of three or more joints, arthritis of the hands, symmetric arthritis, elevated acute phase reactants, elevated rheumatoid factor, and radiologic evidence of RA. These criteria separated inflammatory from non-inflammatory arthritis but were not very specific for RA. It was also not sensitive to early-stage RA, which was a significant drawback. [85] With the development of serologic markers, the diagnostic criteria were redefined. The 2010 American College of Rheumatology and the European League Against Rheumatism (ACR/EULAR) diagnostic criteria for RA are outlined below. It includes four different domains, which are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Hand and Wrist Rheumatoid Arthritis -- History and Physical -- Disease Patterns
retrieved_docs.content: The hallmark of RA is chronic bilateral symmetric inflammatory arthritis (synovitis) involving the small joints of the hands and feet. More than 50% of the cases of RA have an insidious onset of the disease, while abrupt onset can be seen in up to 25% cases. Monoarticular joint involvement, especially that of larger joints such as knee or shoulder that eventually progresses to polyarticular involvement, has been seen. In the elderly population, the onset of RA may mimic symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica with arthralgias, myalgias, and stiffness of the shoulders and hip girdles with elevated ESR and constitutional symptoms such as fever and fatigue. [12] Rarely, extra-articular manifestations, especially rheumatoid nodulosis or interstitial lung disease in addition to seropositivity, can be the initial presenting feature of RA.

retrieved_docs.title: Gamekeeper's Thumb (Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Stener lesion Fracture of the phalanx Rheumatoid arthritis Osteoarthritis

retrieved_docs.title: Monoarticular Arthritis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The most important feature of diagnosis of monoarticular arthritis is to differentiate joint pain from the soft tissue conditions like bursitis or tendinitis that tend to mimic similar symptomology. Timing of symptoms is particularly crucial in this regard. For example, rapid onset of monoarticular pain in seconds or minutes can be signs of trauma, fracture, dislocation/derangement or presence of a loose body in joint. Pain that has onset over several hours to days generally indicates infection or crystal-induced arthritis, whereas pain over days to weeks may be an early sign of chronic conditions like osteoarthritis or rheumatoid arthritis. [18] Certain factors like intravenous drug use or patient on immunosuppression medications can potentially increase the risk of septic arthritis. Triad of symptoms, including urethritis, conjunctivitis, and arthritis after an infection is telltale signs of reactive arthritis. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Arthritis -- Evaluation -- Clinical Presentation and Pathophysiology Related to Individual Viral Entity
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis Viruse s : Hepatitis A sometimes can cause arthralgias but usually no arthritis. Both hepatitis B (HBV) and hepatitis C (HCV) viruses can cause arthritis and arthralgia. Approximately 10% to 25 % of patients with HBV develop symptoms of arthralgia and arthritis. Arthritis in patients with HBV is mediated through the formation and deposition of immune complexes. [4] It can be symmetrical and polyarticular. The symptoms of arthritis usually occur in the prodromal stage of the disease and could be without any other clinical manifestations of hepatitis. The presence of rash and fever may provide clues to the underlying diagnosis. [5] One characteristic feature of HBV-related arthritis is its resolution with the onset of jaundice. [6] Rarely, arthritis can also be seen in HBV-related polyarteritis nodosa. Immune complex formation and deposition are also considered the pathophysiologic mechanism for arthritis in patients with hepatitis C. Arthritis can be seen in at least 2% to 20% of HCV patients. [7] It manifests in two patterns, a  polyarticular, non-erosive, and small joint arthritis resembling RA and an oligoarticular affecting medium and large joints, especially involving ankles, and is associated with mixed cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. In both HBV- and HCV-related arthritis syndromes, RF can be elevated. It is positive in around 25% of cases in HBV and up to 80% in HCV, especially with HCV cryoglobulinemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteitis Condensans Ilii -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Bilateral Asymmetric Gout: This is a crystalline arthropathy most commonly affecting middle-aged men. The most common joints involved are the hands and feet, specifically the first metatarsophalangeal (feet) and radiocarpal (hands). Patients may have elevated serum uric acid levels on laboratory analysis. On imaging, gout is an erosive process, so there should be periarticular erosions, soft tissue swelling, and in some cases, a soft tissue mass (tophus). The demographics, involvement of the hands/feet, and inflammatory changes are key distinguishing features. [15] Psoriatic arthritis (PA): PA is a seronegative inflammatory arthropathy that most commonly affects the hands and feet. Most patients will have a precedent diagnosis of psoriasis with skin manifestations. Unlike gout or rheumatoid arthritis, there is no gender predilection. The involvement of the hands and feet is distal more than proximal. In other words, the interphalangeal joints are more commonly involved than the joints of the midfoot or wrist. There is commonly soft tissue swelling, erosive changes, periostitis, joint subluxations, or ankylosis. The characteristic imaging features and multifocal involvement distinguish it from OSI. [10] [16] [17] Osteoarthritis: The hallmarks of osteoarthritis are sclerosis, osteophyte formation, and subchondral cystic change. Osteoarthritis involvement of the sacroiliac joints will manifest as changes of both sides of the joint with small osteophytes. In addition, it will not have the classic triangular sclerosis seen in OSI.

retrieved_docs.title: Secondary Osteonecrosis of the Knee -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory arthritis: Conditions like rheumatoid arthritis can cause knee pain and bone erosions; however, they typically exhibit distinct clinical and imaging patterns, such as erosions in the juxtaarticular bone and synovitis on MRI.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2579
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine and/or metabolic disorder is classified within the broader category of other non‑traumatic joint disorders. These other non‑traumatic joint disorders are directly related to the condition described in Arabic as اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). Recognizing that endocrine or metabolic‑related arthropathy maps to other non‑traumatic joint disorders and shares a relationship with اعتلال مفصلي helps clinicians place these conditions within a unified framework of joint pathology, facilitating more comprehensive assessment and management of patients with non‑traumatic joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 469
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified under other non‑traumatic joint disorders. This arthropathy has its primary finding site in the joints (klouby) and is linked to the broader concept of joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي). Recognizing that this condition maps to other non‑traumatic joint disorders and involves the joints helps clinicians understand how endocrine or metabolic disturbances can produce non‑traumatic arthropathies that belong to the overall spectrum of joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: Alergeny is described as having the structural class of auranofin, indicating that the chemical framework of alergeny is related to the gold‑containing compound auranofin. Recognizing this structural relationship helps clarify how alergeny may share pharmacologic properties, mechanisms of action, or potential cross‑reactivity with auranofin, which is used in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. Understanding such structural classifications is important for anticipating therapeutic effects, side‑effect profiles, and guiding patient‑specific medication decisions.
